coredata.dc:title: Highly selective molecular imprinting electrochemiluminescence switch sensor for biotoxin L-canavanine measurement
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shuhuai
Li, Jianping
Ma, Xionghui
Liu, Chunhua
Pang, Chaohai
Luo, Jinhui
coredata.dc:description: An electrochemiluminescence (ECL) switch sensor with a dual-recognition mechanism was developed based on complexation reactions and a molecular imprinted film (MIP). The MIP was prepared on a gold electrode with ferroferric oxide/carbon quantum dots (Fe3O4/C-dots) via electrochemical polymerization. An L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex was used as the template molecule and o-phenylenediamine was used as the functional monomer. Further, C-dots were used as the ECL signal elements and the L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex could quench the ECL signal. Subsequently, by using the elution and re-adsorption of the template molecule as the control switch, it was possible to control the ECL intensity of the sensor, and establish a new method for rapidly measuring L-canavanine. A sensor that combined complexation recognition and imprinting recognition demonstrated excellent performance in terms of recognizing specific target molecules. The detection limits of this method reached 7.32 × 10−11 mol L−1, and the measurement results for practical samples were satisfactory.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Switch
Dual-recognition
L-canavanine
Complex

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Fe3O4/C-dots composite and L-canavanine-trinitrobenzene complex
Preparation of sensor decorated with MIP
Electrochemical measurement and ECL measurement methods
Analysis of specific recognition ability of MIP sensor
Analysis of sensor reproducibility and stability

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme showing the construction of MIP-ECL sensor and detection of L-canavanine.
(A) TEM of images of the Fe3O4; (B) TEM of images of Fe3O4/C-dots; (C) XRD spectrogram of Fe3O4/C-dots; (D) hysteresis curves of nanoparticles: a. Fe3O4; b. Fe3O4/C-dots.
CVs of the electrochemical polymerization of o-phenylenediamine and L-canavanine-trinitrobenzene complex, scan rate: 50 mV s−1.

Characterization of MIP sensor: (A) CV: a. bare gold electrode; b. Fe3O4/C-dots-modified electrode; c. MIP-modified electrode; d. MIP-modified electrode after template molecule removal; e. MIP electrode after re-adsorption of 1.5 × 10−9 mol L−1 template molecule; f. NIP-modified electrode; g. NIP-modified electrode after template molecule removal; (B) EIS: a. bare gold electrode; b. Fe3O4/C-dots-modified electrode; c. MIP-modified electrode; d. MIP-modified electrode after template molecule removal; e. MIP electrode after re-adsorption of 1.5 × 10−9 mol L−1 template molecule. (C) Adsorption isotherm of template molecule on MIP and NIP.
ECL response of MIP sensor: a. C-dots; b. Fe3O4/C-dots-modified electrode; c. MIP-modified electrode; d. MIP-modified electrode after template molecule removal.
(A) Response time optimization: a. elution time; b. re-adsorption time; (B) effect of pH.

ECL response of MIP sensor in different conditions: a. MIP sensor after L-canavanine template molecule removal; b. MIP sensor re-adsorption of L-canavanine complex; c. MIP sensor re-adsorption of L-arginine complex; d. MIP sensor re-adsorption of L-canavanine containing L-arginine complex; e. MIP sensor re-adsorption of L-canavanine complex containing other amino acids and common ions. f. MIP sensor without trinitrobenzene and re-adsorption of L-canavanine containing L-arginine; g. MIP sensor without trinitrobenzene and re-adsorption of L-canavanine containing L-arginine; h. MIP sensor without trinitrobenzene and re-adsorption of other amino acids and common ions.
ECL of MIP sensor with L-canavanine adsorbed from solutions with different concentrations. a–n: (0, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 150, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000, and 1200) × 10−10 mol L−1 L-canavanine.
Results of sample assay and recoveries.

head.ce:title: Highly selective molecular imprinting electrochemiluminescence switch sensor for biotoxin L-canavanine measurement

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Haikou 571101, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Haikou, 571101, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Quality and Safety for Tropical Fruits and Vegetables, Haikou 571101, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Quality and Safety for Tropical Fruits and Vegetables

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hainan Provincial Key Laboratory of Quality and Safety for Tropical Fruits and Vegetables, Haikou, 571101, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin 541004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences, Haikou 571101, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analysis and Test Center of Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrochemiluminescence (ECL) switch sensor with a dual-recognition mechanism was developed based on complexation reactions and a molecular imprinted film (MIP). The MIP was prepared on a gold electrode with ferroferric oxide/carbon quantum dots (Fe3O4/C-dots) via electrochemical polymerization. An L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex was used as the template molecule and o-phenylenediamine was used as the functional monomer. Further, C-dots were used as the ECL signal elements and the L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex could quench the ECL signal. Subsequently, by using the elution and re-adsorption of the template molecule as the control switch, it was possible to control the ECL intensity of the sensor, and establish a new method for rapidly measuring L-canavanine. A sensor that combined complexation recognition and imprinting recognition demonstrated excellent performance in terms of recognizing specific target molecules. The detection limits of this method reached 7.32 × 10−11 mol L−1, and the measurement results for practical samples were satisfactory.

ce:section.ce:para: L-canavanine [1] is a natural nonprotein amino acid widely found in leguminous plants and their seeds. L-canavanine has a certain level of toxicity and, as a common biotoxin, its over-ingestion by humans or animals could lead to it being substituted for arginine in protein synthesis. Because L-canavanine and arginine have different structures, the protein synthesized in such a case would have unusual functions and would ultimately cause death [2]. On the other hand, because L-canavanine could replace arginine and enter cells to synthesize proteins with physiological defects, it has antineoplastic property is significant, and has become a popular focus of study in tumor research. Therefore, for both health and safety and medical research, it is important to establish a highly sensitive, selective, efficient, and quick method for measuring L-canavanine. Currently, the primary method for measuring L-canavanine is high pressure liquid chromatography [3,4]. However, this method has poor sensitivity, requires expensive equipment, and is not suitable for measuring complex substrate samples (particularly samples involving L-arginine interference). Thus, it does not satisfy the need for precise measurement of trace amounts of L-canavanine.

Molecular imprinting polymers (MIP) are of increasing interest owing to their potential applications in molecular recognition. Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) sensors based on MIP (MIP-ECL) [5,6] have high sensitivity, and selectivity; this is an important research area in analytical chemistry and used for detecting various molecules. MIP-ECL sensors can simultaneously measure multiple components and specifically recognize molecules, while also showing high stability and wide practicability. Thus, such sensors have unique advantages for applications involving food safety [7], environmental monitoring [8], and drug analysis [9]. Common MIP-ECL sensors are based primarily on methods that recognize specific sites in MIP [10,11]. This facilitates the recognition of target molecules, and produces excellent specific recognition ability. However, traditional MIP technology is overly reliant on the recognition site function of MIP. Because of the limited number of sites and the relative instability of the imprinting materials, its specific recognition properties would inevitably be influenced. However, by combining MIP with other recognition techniques, it has become possible to efficiently increase the number of recognition sites in sensors, eliminate interference, and increase selectivity. This research group previously reported introducing supramolecular recognition and DNA aptamer recognition technologies into MIP sensor production, to further enhance MIP selectivity [12,13]. Further studies have shown that the complexation process is also a molecule recognition process [14]. By controlling the complexation conditions, the target molecules were selectively recognized. Complexation [15] is a complex formation process involving the interaction between electron donors and acceptors. Compared with supramolecular recognition and DNA aptamer recognition technology, experimental complexation processes are simpler and less expensive. Additionally, complexation specificity can be controlled and MIP selection capabilities can be increased. Therefore, in combination with complexation, a dual-recognition effect MIP sensor could realize highly sensitive and selective recognition measurements of a target molecule. Previous studies have not reported the use of an MIP sensor for measuring L-canavanine, particularly with respect to the dual-recognition effects of an MIP sensor combining two recognition techniques.

In this study, an L-canavanine-trinitrobenzene complex was used as a template molecule. O-phenylenediamine was used as a functional monomer, and electrochemical polymerization was employed to study and build an MIP sensor for L-canavanine on a gold electrode decorated with ferroferric oxide/carbon quantum dots (Fe3O4/C-dots). To utilize this sensor to measure L-canavanine, as L-canavanine reacts with trinitrobenzene to form the L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex, trinitrobenzene was added to the test solution. This was the first recognition of L-canavanine using the complex. Subsequently, the MIP was used to perform a second recognition of the newly formed L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex to achieve dual recognition of L-canavanine. The dual-recognition system could enhance the L-canavanine selectivity of the sensor. In addition, this study introduced Fe3O4/C-dots as the ECL signal, which could be quenched by the L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex. Thus, it was possible to consider the adsorption and elution as the control switch for the ECL signal, and establish a new method for measuring L-canavanine sensitively. The experimental principle is shown in Fig. 1 .

L-canavanine was purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. (Shanghai) and trinitrobenzene and o-phenylenediamine were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. L-arginine and H2O2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Unless otherwise specified, the drugs used in the experiments were analytical reagents, and the water was twice-deionized water.

A CHI-660E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chen Hua Instrument Co., Ltd.); an MPI-E type electrochemiluminescence analysis system (Xi'an Remex Analysis Instruments Co., Ltd); and a three-electrode system were used. A gold electrode decorated with an MIP (d = 2 mm) was used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl was used as the reference electrode, and a platinum electrode was used as the counter electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Fe3O4/C-dots composite and L-canavanine-trinitrobenzene complex
ce:section.ce:para: Using methods described in previous studies [16,17], 0.05 g of nano-graphite was weighed (as described above), and then 10 mL concentrated sulfuric acid and 10 mL concentrated nitric acid were added. Oxidation was allowed to proceed at room temperature for 12 h, after which the mixture was placed in an ultrasound instrument for 12 h. Finally, the mixture was placed in a furnace connected to an exhaust gas recovery device, where it was burned for 20 min at 250 °C to remove the concentrated sulfuric acid and concentrated nitric acid. The product was then re-dispersed into 50 mL of deionized water, and the resulting black suspension liquid was filtered via suction through a 0.22 μm millipore filter membrane, to obtain a pale yellow filtrate. The filtrate was further dialyzed in dialysis bags for 5 h to obtain a pure C-dots solution. C-dots powder was freeze-dried from the C-dots solution in a lyophilizer for later use. In accordance with procedures described in the literature, Fe2+ and Fe3+ were added to the alkaline medium at a mole ratio of 2:1, and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles were synthesized via chemical co-precipitation. At 35 °C, FeSO4·7H2O and FeCl3·6H2O were mixed. When the temperature reached 50 °C, the solution was rapidly stirred while adding 1 mol L−1 NaOH solution, and the pH was adjusted to 11. Then, 0.01 g of C-dots powder was added, and the mixture was heated to 80 °C while stirring for 1 h. N2 flows were maintained throughout the experimental process. The black suspension subsequently obtained was separated using a magnet, and twice-deionized water and ethanol were used for cleaning. Finally, the suspension of Fe3O4/C-dots was dispersed in 2 mL twice-deionized water.

Next, 0.5 mL of L-canavanine standard solution (or sample solution) and 20 μL of trinitrobenzene were added to 0.5 mL of sodium citrate buffer solution (0.05 mol L−1, pH 9.0), and shaken well. The solution was placed into a 50 °C water bath to avoid light exposure for 30 min. The obtained L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex was cooled to room temperature (for further use) using flowing water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of sensor decorated with MIP
ce:section.ce:para: Before using the gold electrodes, their surfaces were ground and polished using 1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 μm Al2O3. Then, the electrodes were placed into double distilled water, ethanol, and 50% sulfuric acid for ultrasonic cleaning and airing for future use. After applying 10 μL Fe3O4/C-dots on the gold electrode via dripping, they were stored in a 4 °C refrigerator and protected from light for 2 h. Then, the electrodes were cleaned using twice-deionized water for future use. The electrochemical polymerization process was performed by placing an electrode decorated with Fe3O4/C-dots into 5 mL 0.04 mol L−1 acetic acid/sodium acetate buffer solution (pH = 5.0, with 0.5 mL L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex solution and 1 × 10−3 mol L−1 o-phenylenediamine). The cyclic voltammetry scan range was 0–0.8 V, and the scan speed was 50 mV s−1 with 20 cycles. After polymerization, the electrode was placed in an ethanol-acetic acid mixed solution (1:1) to elute the L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex and other adsorbed substances. The MIP was obtained by imprinting holes containing the retained specific recognition sites. The same method was used to prepare non-molecular imprinting membranes (NIP). The only difference was that the L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex was not added in the polymerization process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical measurement and ECL measurement methods
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical measurements were conducted using a 3 × 10−4 mol L−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] probe (with 0.5 mol L−1 KCL). The following conditions were used for cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements: scan range of 0–0.6 V, and scan speed of 50 mV s−1. The following conditions were used for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS): electric potential of 0.190 V, frequency range of 0.1–100,000 Hz, and an alternating voltage of 5 mV. The ECL intensity was measured using a three-electrode system in 0.1 mol L−1 Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH = 7.8, with 1 mmol L−1 H2O2). The scan range was −0.2–0.6 V (vs SCE), and scan speed was 100 mV s−1. The maximum voltage of the photomultiplier was 800 V, the sampling rate was 10 T s−1, the magnification ratio was 3, and the measurement time was 90 s.

The bean callus was placed in a mortar and ground directly. It was allowed to stand and then centrifuged for 2 min at 10,000 rpm to obtain the supernatant. After rotary evaporation and concentration, the L-canavanine extract solution was obtained for future use.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and hysteresis regression curves were used to analyze the Fe3O4/C-dots composite nanomaterials. As shown in Fig. 2A and B, the Fe3O4/C-dots nanomaterial is spherical [18]. It is dispersed very well; the C-dots cover the particle surface of Fe3O4 and inhibit the agglomeration of Fe3O4. The diameter of the nanoparticles is approximately 40 nm. The XRD spectrogram in Fig.2Cshows a wide dispersion peak at approximately 2θ (at approximately 16.3°) corresponding to amorphous C. The five characteristic peaks (2θ = 30.4°, 35.7°, 43.4°, 53.5°, and 62.7°) represent the five crystal faces in the cubic phase of Fe3O4, namely (220), (331), (400), (422), and (440) [19]. Further, from the hysteresis regression curve in Fig. 2D, it can be seen that the specific saturation magnetization of the Fe3O4/C-dots is approximately 37.8, which is less than 62.7 (the value for pure Fe3O4) [20]. This indicates that the magnetism of Fe3O4 decreases after being covered by C-dots while still having superparamagnetic characteristics. The above results indicate the successful fabrication of an Fe3O4/C-dots composite nanomaterial.

The electrochemical polymerization of o-phenylenediamine on the surface of a gold electrode (decorated with Fe3O4/C-dots) is an irreversible process, as shown in Fig. 3 . There is an obvious oxidation peak at approximately 0.4 V. As the polymerization proceeds, the peak current decreases continuously. When the polymerization circles reached 20, the peak current decreased to an extremely small level, which means that a compact MIP layer had already polymerized on the electrode surface.

Because o-phenylenediamine and L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complexes do not produce oxidation and reduction peaks on gold electrodes within a range of −0.2–0.6 V, K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] was used as a probe to characterize the responses between electrodes and substrate solutions. First, cyclic voltammetry was used to characterize the MIP. The expedited properties of imprinting pores on MIP influenced electron transfers. As shown in Fig. 4A, when Fe3O4/C-dots were formed on the surface of a gold electrode, electron transfer was impeded to some extent. The peak current decreased slightly compared to that for a bare gold electrode (from curve a to curve b). After the formation of a compact MIP on the electrode surface, the peak current decreased dramatically (curve c). After the elution of a template molecule complex, the imprinting pore was opened and the current increased (curve d). When the target molecule was re-absorbed into the MIP, the imprinting pore was closed again and the current decreased again (from curve d to curve e). Similarly, for the NIP, because there was no template molecule during the polymerization process, an imprinting pore did not appear after elution. Therefore, the current changed only slightly (from curve f to curve g). The above results show that the MIP responds specifically to template molecules. Similarly, the EIS method can be used to characterize resistance variations on electrode surfaces and further obtain resistance variation conditions for these surfaces. The inset in Fig. 4B shows the inset showed the Randles equivalent circuit model used for EIS analysis [21]. The total electrode impedance corresponds to the electron transfer resistance (Ret) in series with the parallel connection of the double layer capacitance (Cdl) and Warburg impedance (Zw). In EIS, the semicircle diameter of EIS equals the electron transfer resistance, Ret. When the surface of a gold electrode is decorated with Fe3O4/C-dots and MIP, the conditions of the electrode surface are changed. This impedes electron transfer on the electrode surface, which results in the resistance value increasing from about 78.6 Ω to 144.2 Ω (from curve a to curve b). After the formation of a compact MIP on the electrode surface, the resistance value increased dramatically to about 2407.5 Ω (curve d). After the elution of template molecules, the resistance value on electrode surfaces decreased to175.1 Ω (curve c). After re-adsorption, however, the resistance value once again increased to 1330.0 Ω (curve e). The above result shows that an MIP sensor was successfully produced. Moreover, the binding and selectivity to L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene of MIP were evaluated by batch adsorption experiments. As shown in Fig. 4B, the adsorption capacity of MIP was 28.39 μmol g−1 and NIP was 1.89 μmol g−1.

The ECL response of the nanomaterial was studied. As shown in Fig. 5 , C-dots on a gold electrode surface generated an obvious ECL signal (curve a). In contrast, the ECL signal of the Fe3O4/C-dots was much stronger than that of the C-dots (curve b). This is because Fe3O4 has the greater specific area, and excellent conductivity. It provided a high-speed path for electron transfer between the electrode and C-dots, which means C-dots were activated effectively. When the MIP layer polymerized on the Fe3O4/C-dots decorating the gold electrode surface, the ECL signal of the sensor decreased dramatically (curve c). This occurred because the L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex sufficiently quenched the luminescent intensity of the C-dots. This quenching may have occurred because the C-dots are activated and reacted with the co-reactant H2O2 under a certain voltage. Then, further reactions take place and the C-dots reach an excited state, producing optical radiation when they return to the ground state. The possible ECL mechanisms can be expressed as in Schemes (1)–(3) [22,23]:
(1)
C − dots +
ne −
→ nC −
dots −
(2)
2C −
dots −
+
H 2
O 2
→ 2C −
dots ∗
+
2OH −
(3)
nC −
dots ∗
→ C − dots + h v

In this case, C-dots− and C-dots⁎ refer to the reduced species and excited states of C-dots, respectively. Trinitrobenzene as an electron acceptor, directly accepted the electrons in Scheme (1), and the electron cannot react with C-dots further, thus impeding the production of luminescence. After the elution of template molecules, this impeding function disappears and the luminescent intensity recovers (curve d).

Experiments were conducted to determine how signal measurement was affected by the elution time of the template molecule, re-adsorption time, and pH values of the buffer solution. As shown in Fig. 6A, during elution, the template molecule complex is eliminated from the MIP, the switch is opened, and the response signal is enhanced. After an elution time of 3 min, the ECL signal reaches a maximum value and does not change, and the elution process is completed; the elution efficiency of MIP was 92.5%. When the template molecule (1.20 × 10−8 mol L−1) is re-adsorbed on the MIP, the switch is turned off, and the ECL signal decreases. When the adsorption time reaches 4 min, the ECL signal reaches a minimum value and does not change, and the re-adsorption process is completed. Therefore, the optimal elution time is 3 min, and the optimal re-adsorption time is 4 min. The effects of Tris-HCI buffer solutions with different pH values on ECL response were also studied. The results are shown in Fig. 6B. When the pH values change from 7.4 to 7.8, the ECL response increases with an increase in pH values. For pH values greater than 7.8, the ECL signal of the sensor will continuously decreases with increasing pH values. Therefore, a pH of 7.8 was used for the ECL response of the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of specific recognition ability of MIP sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sensor's ability to specifically recognize L-canavanine was evaluated; additionally, the interference of structural analogues such as L-arginine with sensor selectivity was also studied. L-canavanine (1.50 × 10−9 mol L−1) and L-arginine (1.50 × 10−7 mol L−1) were used to prepare complex; the same experimental steps followed to examine the ECL response were used. The results in Fig. 7 indicated that L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene can adsorbed by MIP and quench ECL intensity (from a to b). However, L-arginine–trinitrobenzene can't be recognized by MIP, so the ECL intensity has little change after readsorption step (from a to c). The effects of other amino acids and common ions on the ECL response signals of the system were also analyzed. The results indicated that, 400-fold (relative to the concentration of L-canavanine) glycine, alanine, valine, Leucine, tryptophan, proline, cysteine, serine, tyrosine, asparaginate, glutamine; and 500-fold (relative to the concentration of L-canavanine) Na+, K+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, NH4 +, Cd2+, Ca2+, Cl−, F−, I−, Br−, NO3 −, CO3 2−, SO3 2−, SO4 2− and PO4 3−, did not interfere with quantitative determination of L-canavanine. This result shows the excellent L-canavanine selectivity of the system.

In addition, the same method was used to analyze the selectivity of traditional sensors. The results are shown in Fig. 7. L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene containing L-arginine (from b to d) or other amino acids and common ions (from b to e) have no interference on the ECL response signals for this sensor. However, the traditional molecular imprinting sensor (using L-canavanine as the template molecule instead of the complex) is greatly interfered by L-arginine (from f to g) and the abovementioned interfering substances (from f to h). This indirectly shows that the sensor developed in this experiment shows significantly improved specific selectivity. This is because of the selectivity of the traditional MIP sensors is not completely specific to L-canavanine, and several other interfering substances are also adsorbed on the MIP via physical adsorption. However, this sensor combined complexation and imprinting recognitions, MIP and trinitrobenzene provide more recognition sites for L-canavanine. This will dramatically reduce non-specific interference.

Under optimal conditions, the sensor was placed in an L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex solution with different concentrations for reaction and the ECL signals examined. As shown in Fig. 8 , the ECL intensity of the system decreases with an increase in the L-canavanine adsorbed on the MIP. The decreasing values of the ECL (ΔI) and L-canavanine concentration (c) show a linear relationship, when the concentrations are in the range of 2.00 × 10−10 − 1.20 × 10−7 mol L−1. The linear regression equation is ΔI = 2.06 c (10−10 mol L−1) + 4.95 × 10−9, the correlation coefficient is 0.9983, and the method detection limit is 7.32 × 10−11 mol L−1 (D.L. = KSb / a, K = 3). Simultaneously, using L-canavanine as the template molecule on the Fe3O4/C-dots, the surface of the gold electrode surface (without introducing trinitrobenzene to form a complex) was modified and the MIP was formed using MIP production methods to obtain a traditional sensor. The ECL response signal of L-canavanine was identified. The results show that its linear range is 2.00 × 10−9–1.00 × 10−7 mol L−1, and the detection limit is 1.23 × 10−9 mol L−1. The above results indicate that the introduced trinitrobenzene complex improved the recognition properties of the sensor, owing to better detection ranges and limits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of sensor reproducibility and stability
ce:section.ce:para: Sensor reproducibility experiments were conducted by measuring ECL signals after the reaction of the sensor with the 1.50 × 10−9 mol L−1 L-canavanine. First, for the same sensor, ECL signals were continuously measured five times, with a relative standard deviation of 2.14%. Subsequently, the ECL signals of five sensors were measured. The relative standard deviation of the ECL response was 3.34%. The above results show that the sensor has high reproducibility. These sensors should be stored in 4 °C refrigerators to protect them from light exposure when not in use. To measure the stability of the sensor, the variations in ECL responses were examined periodically. The ECL signal intensity changed only slightly within 10 days; it decreased by 8.62% after 20 days. The electrode reuse times were also obtained by measuring the ECL signals after the reaction of the sensor on the 1.50 × 10−9 mol L−1 L-canavanine. After the sensor was reused 30 times, the ECL signal intensity decreased by only 11.8%. The above results show that the sensor has high stability.

Sample measurements were performed using experimental methods, along with spike recovery experiments. The results are presented in Table 1 . The recovery rate for the spike recovery experiments is 90.0%–116%, which is satisfactory.

An L-canavanine–trinitrobenzene complex recognition property was introduced in the production of molecular imprinting sensors. After combining Fe3O4/C-dots, a highly sensitive L-canavanine sensor capable of specifically recognizing a biotoxin was obtained. Because the two molecule recognition techniques provide specific recognition sites, they significantly improve the recognition ability of the sensor. During L-canavanine measurement, interference by complex substances can be eliminated. The combined dual-recognition mechanism developed in this study provides a theoretical and practical reference for the specific sensitive measurements of antibiotics, residual veterinary drugs, biotoxins, and toxic harmful substances. These methods can be widely applied by varying the underlying template molecules.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Central Public-interest Scientific Institution Basal Research Fund for Chinese Academy of Tropical Agricultural Sciences (No. 1630082017001, 1630082017002 and 1630082019003) and the Natural Science Foundation of Hainan Province of China (No. 217222).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: l-Canavanine: a higher plant insecticidal allelochemical
Amino Acids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Rosenthal, l-Canavanine: a higher plant insecticidal allelochemical, Amino Acids 21 (2001) 319-330.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dynamin 3 suppresses growth and induces apoptosis of hepatocellular carcinoma cells by activating inducible nitric oxide synthase production
Oncol. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Gu, J. Yao, P. Sun, Dynamin 3 suppresses growth and induces apoptosis of hepatocellular carcinoma cells by activating inducible nitric oxide synthase production, Oncol. Lett. 13 (2017) 4776-4784.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: HPLC analysis of canavanine and canaline in Canavalia ensiformis, and in excreta and serum of chicks
J. Sci. Food Agric.
